designProjects.title: Sustainable Outdoor Gear Using Knit and Woven Fabrics: A Case of Shell Jacket
designProjects.description: Sustainability and minimalism are increasingly emphasized in the fast-changing fashion industry, with consumers now favoring timeless and simple designs. Currently, the market mostly adopts woven fabrics that are windproof and waterproof, but these need improvement in terms of comfort and adaptability. Knitted fabrics are known for their superior comfort and adaptability and are widely used in daily apparel due to their excellent comfort and stretch. However, there is still much room for their application in outdoor apparel. This project explores the design of an outdoor shell jacket that combines knit and woven technologies, utilizing eco-friendly and other functional materials to meet consumer and market demands for sustainable fashion while demonstrating the advantages of versatility and adaptability to fashion trends.

designProjects.title: Blooming Bayou: A Louisiana Wetland-Inspired Fashion Design Based on Native Floral Imagery
designProjects.description: Louisiana's wetland ecosystems are characterized by diverse native flora, rich biodiversity, and distinctive regional landscapes. These natural environments not only shape Louisiana's ecological identity but also provide abundant inspiration for artistic and design practices. Guided by Biophilic Design Theory, this project explores how native wetland flowers can be translated into contemporary fashion design. Inspired by the soft ivory petals, layered textures, and graceful growth patterns of a Louisiana wetland flower, the design transforms floral characteristics into garment silhouette, material selection, and textile embellishment. An A-line silhouette, flowing fabric strips, and three-dimensional floral details were employed to capture the flower's natural movement and delicate structure. By integrating ecological observation with fashion design, this project demonstrates how regional natural imagery can be transformed into wearable forms that foster connections among the body, nature, and local culture while contributing to place-based sustainable design narratives.

designProjects.title: Reimagining the Chiton: Advancing Zero-Waste Knitwear through Historical Inspiration and Digital Innovation
designProjects.description: The global fashion industry produces approximately 92 million tons of textile waste each year. Much waste comes from fabric offcuts generated during traditional garment production. As sustainability becomes a global concern, knitting technology offers a new solution. Unlike woven garments, which require cutting and sewing, knitting enables yarns to be directly formed into three-dimensional garment shapes. For instance, Shima Seiki's Whole Garment technology uses computer programming to make clothes in one step, with no cutting and less labor. This zero-waste method aligns with broader efforts to decouple fashion production from environmental degradation. Interestingly, the zero-waste principle embedded in modern knitting can be traced to historical practices. One notable example is the ancient Greek chiton, a minimalist garment made from one piece of rectangular fabric draped and secured at the shoulders without cutting or sewing. This project explores how digital knitting can bring together the minimalism of the chiton and the need for sustainable design.

designProjects.title: Venus Fold: A Knitted Translation of Sculptural Drapery
designProjects.description: Traditional garment construction often relies on cutting, sewing, and shaping processes to create three-dimensional forms. Knitting technology offers an alternative approach by enabling structure, texture, and form to be generated directly within the fabric. Inspired by the drapery of the Venus de Milo, this project explores how sculptural folds can be translated into contemporary knitwear. Through SHIMA SEIKI jacquard knitting technology, grayscale imagery of the sculpture was transformed into knitted patterns that combine elastic and non-elastic yarns to create tension-driven pleats and draped structures. The resulting garment merges flat knitted construction with sculptural volume while maintaining material efficiency through rectangular pattern layouts. This project demonstrates the potential of knitting structures and material interactions to generate expressive forms and expands the possibilities of sustainable knitwear design through structural innovation.

designProjects.title: HOW TO LET "LIGHT" PASS THROUGH THE WINDOW
Structural Study and Innovative Design of a Tang Dynasty Double-Breasted Standing-Collar Short-Sleeved Robe from the Haixi Prefecture Ethnic Museum
designProjects.description:
Ancient garments preserved in archaeological and museum collections provide valuable evidence for understanding historical clothing systems, technological development, and cultural exchange. The double-breasted standing-collar short-sleeved robe housed in the Haixi Prefecture Ethnic Museum represents a rare example of Tang Dynasty dress discovered along the Qinghai Route of the Silk Road. Influenced by both Central Plains clothing traditions and local ethnic cultures, the garment reflects the multicultural interactions that characterized northwestern China during the Tang Dynasty.

This research investigates the robe's structure, cutting system, decorative motifs, and cultural origins through garment measurement, pattern reconstruction, experimental archaeology, comparative analysis, and 3D virtual restoration. Particular attention is given to its unique standing-collar construction, cross-shaped cutting method, and the relationship between garment structure and wearer comfort. Motif analysis further reveals the integration of dragon, phoenix, roundel, and geometric patterns associated with both Tang Dynasty and Persian artistic traditions.

By combining historical research with digital reconstruction and stress testing, this study contributes to a deeper understanding of Tang Dynasty costume culture, minority dress traditions, and cultural exchange along the Silk Road. Furthermore, it provides a foundation for the preservation, reinterpretation, and innovative design of traditional Chinese garments in contemporary contexts.

designProjects.title: THE CHARMD CHINA INTERNATIONAL UNDERWEAR CREATIVE CONTEST 2021
designProjects.description:
Collection: I WANT TO BE A "MACARON" GIRL.

This collection is inspired by the vibrant colors of macarons, aiming to provide more options for energetic women in the post-pandemic era. The designs seek to blend athletic functionality with sensuality and elegance, allowing women to feel both confident and graceful while staying active.

designProjects.title: EXPLORE BOY
designProjects.description: An undergraduate menswear design project that explores boyhood, identity, and deconstructed garment forms through the collection EXPLORE BOY.
